This technique is based on the QED effect, that is, the quantum mechanical scattering of light by a plane wavefront. This work constitutes an important starting point for further studies in this area, such as: (i) the practical development of methods to control optical phenomena, using one or more methods of optics, preferably including plasmonics and inorganic nanofabrication; (ii) the exploitation of QED effects, especially for the improvement of optoelectronic devices; and (iii) the realization of new holographic concepts, such as nonlinear colloidal opto-mechanics, in order to improve the properties of QED sources. The resulting results are of immediate importance, firstly for the scientific community in identifying new fundamental scientific advances and applications; secondly, for the industry producing lasers, high-voltage devices, and optical components, especially those which require low power consumption in order to produce them in quantities consistent with the expected demand (for example, medical or telecommunications); and finally, for the exploitation of semiconducting devices in different physical regimes and for different applications, for example, for electronic devices or for biosensors. text: Anne Spencer was born on October 20, 1918, at St. Albans, in Hertfordshire. At the age of seventeen she married John Edward, at the Abbey Church in St. Albans, and they had six children, of whom only two survived to maturity: two daughters, Margaret and Anne. Mrs. Spencer taught English in a private setting and at Holy Trinity School for Girls, which had been founded by Archbishop Thomas Vaughan, in 1939. As headmistress of the school, she had begun to research the history of English institutions and the architectural and decorative art of the Tudors. By 1970 she had become a professor of history of architecture at the College of King's in London. Her academic skills were soon noticed, and it was not yet clear whether she would continue to teach at academic institutions or work in the field of social rehabilitation. In 1979 she retired and was appointed president emeritus of the St. Paul's Chapel, Aldgate East, and, in January 1982, a Dame Principal, the last step below headmistress. In 1985 her husband died, leaving one son, Mark. In February 2003, her husband Lord Thomas of Cwmgiedd Hall purchased the home from the Bishop family, which had owned it since 1785. The new home was announced a few weeks later, but the plan was withdrawn due to opposition from the local population and London council members, whose leadership was led by Ken Livingstone. In December 1997, however, Lady Catherine Ashton announced that the application for the new home would be submitted; it is thought that the project is now awaiting a building permit. The construction of the home began in November of the year 2000, and it was completed and opened in 2005. Despite the long delay in the construction of the home, which was the first home built with modern materials, it was praised for its spacious and sophisticated open-plan design, which allows the residents to descend the grand staircase from the upper floor of the house, while inside the layout is simple and efficient. The same design, with a few minor changes, has been praised in several recent reviews, including those by Jonathan Ive, designer of the ipod. The interior is rich in original features; the painter Paul Eriksson was commissioned to make beautiful reproductions of paintings he saw in the residence of St. Clement Danes, St. Peter's Church Cathedral, the Palace of Westminster and other royal properties, including the Chamber of Queen Elizabeth II in Windsor Castle and the George V Room in the Tower Bridge. Among other things, a great deal of attention has been paid to re-creating the ornate tapestries of the women's headdresses, some of which are estimated to be worth millions. In May 2007, the Royal Exchange Gallery will hold an exhibition of Ms. Spencer's works.
